mnemonics.title: Warshall's Algorithm Steps
mnemonics.content: K-P-R: Keep (keep old value), Path through k (if possible), Replace (with new value if shorter/better)

mnemonics.title: Floyd's Formula
mnemonics.content: MIN-K-J: Min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j]) - always take the minimum path through k

mnemonics.title: Transitive Closure Meaning
mnemonics.content: If A→B and B→C, then A→C must exist - CLOSURE fills in all these transitive connections

acronyms.expansion: Reachability matrix entry from vertex i to vertex j
acronyms.usage: In Warshall's algorithm, R[i][j]=1 means vertex i can reach vertex j

acronyms.expansion: Distance matrix entry representing shortest path from i to j
acronyms.usage: In Floyd's algorithm, stores the minimum distance found so far

acronyms.expansion: Infinity - represents no path exists between vertices
acronyms.usage: Used to initialize non-existent edges in Floyd's algorithm

visualTips.visualization: Think of a 3D cube where k is height layer, i is row, j is column - you visit every cell in the cube
Picture vertex k as a 'middle point' - you go from i to k, then k to j, checking if this is shorter than direct i to j
Imagine filling a matrix like a puzzle - each iteration k adds new reachable pairs, gradually completing the picture
Watch the distance matrix transform from adjacency matrix to shortest path matrix through n stages

analogies.analogy: Like building a network of flight connections - if you can fly A→B and B→C, you can now fly A→C directly in the route map
Like finding the shortest driving route between all city pairs by considering all possible stopovers at each city
k acts like a 'referee' or 'middleman' - you check if going through them gives a better result than the direct path
Like a rumor spreading network - if A tells B, and B tells C, then A's rumor eventually reaches C
